sections.question: During January 1977 employees of the Franchise Tax Board submitted petitions to the Board requesting an amendment to the Department's Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code section 87307. On February 10, 1977 the requestor asked for copies of the petitions submitted by the employees pursuant to Government Code section 81008. On February 16, 1977 the Franchise Tax Board denied the request stating that Government Code section 51005 referred only to financial statements required by law and did not extend to voluntary statements filed by employees seeking administrative review of their designated status. The requestor asked that the Board be directed to immediately comply with the provisions of Government Code section 81008.
sections.conclusion: Petitions, whether formal or informal, filed with an agency pursuant to Government Code section 87307, are not 'statements' or 'reports' within Government Code section 81008. The Franchise Tax Board's view that petitions for modification or removal from a code are an internal personnel matter until such time as the Board decides to act upon the petitions is both reasonable and permissible. However, if the Board determines to act on petitions and proposes amendments, a public hearing must be held, and all petitions submitted to the FPPC calendared for hearing will be open for public inspection for a reasonable period prior to any hearing.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether employee petitions requesting amendments to a Conflict of Interest Code are public records subject to inspection under Government Code section 81008, or whether they are internal personnel matters exempt from disclosure.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that employee petitions for amendments to a Conflict of Interest Code are not public records under Government Code section 81008, and may be treated as internal personnel matters. However, if the Board proposes amendments based on petitions, a public hearing is required and petitions will become available for public inspection at that time.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains incoming request (February 22, 1977 letter from Gleed), FPPC response (March 3, 1977 letter from Spurlock), and supporting materials including Franchise Tax Board denial letter. Some text is difficult to read due to image quality, but main legal question and conclusion are clear. The document ID '77ADV-77-125' and advice letter classification are accurate.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: During January 1977 employees of the Franchise Tax Board submitted petitions to the Board requesting an amendment to the Department's Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code section 87307. On February 10, 1977 the requestor asked for copies of the petitions submitted by the employees pursuant to Government Code section 81008. On February 16, 1977 the Franchise Tax Board denied the request stating that Government Code section 51005 referred only to financial statements required by law and did not extend to voluntary statements filed by employees seeking administrative review of their designated status. The requestor asked that the Board be directed to immediately comply with the provisions of Government Code section 81008.

CONCLUSION: Petitions, whether formal or informal, filed with an agency pursuant to Government Code section 87307, are not 'statements' or 'reports' within Government Code section 81008. The Franchise Tax Board's view that petitions for modification or removal from a code are an internal personnel matter until such time as the Board decides to act upon the petitions is both reasonable and permissible. However, if the Board determines to act on petitions and proposes amendments, a public hearing must be held, and all petitions submitted to the FPPC calendared for hearing will be open for public inspection for a reasonable period prior to any hearing.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advised that employee petitions requesting amendments to the Franchise Tax Board's Conflict of Interest Code are internal personnel matters not subject to mandatory public disclosure under Government Code section 81008. The FPPC affirmed the Board's position while clarifying that if proposed amendments result, public hearings must be held and petitions become subject to inspection.
